It is now a long time back since we did some “ prying,”’
as Captain Wade-Thompson, the Pervert Priests’ Pro-
tector, might call it, into the Rathmines Council. We
laid its traditional Irish Protestant “ non-sectarianism '’
bare, and thoe establishment of the Rathmines and Rath-
gar Catholic Association was a direct outcome of our
exposures.

Wo see that a young man on the staff of Buff, the
ha’penny evening run from the offices of Lord Bung’s
Orange Lzpress, has been interviewing the child-like and
bland Chairman of the premier bigot District Council of
Ireland. The account of the interview with old Mr.
Edmundson commences with the following sentence:—
 As might have been expected, a good deal of interest
is being taken in the question of the rates in Rathmines,
for it is one that dives deep down into the pockets of the
ratepayers.”’ We should think so. A rise of about
1s. 9d. in the £ is calculated to interest ratepayers, if
not to add to the—if any—popularity of old Mr. Ed-
mundson and his “ Saved ’’ colleagues who nobble by the
two-ward “ ticket *’ system the local government of Rath-
mines. Messrs. Edmundson and Co’s. failures in Par-
liament have cost about £10,000, and the ratepayers,
who are certainly not represented by the bigot and exclu-
sive clique who mismanage Rathmines, have now to pay
for this wasteful and ridiculous expenditure on failures.
Said Mr. Edmundson to Mr. Buff’s young man—*“T
have the greatest sympathy for many of the smaller pro-
perty-holders on whom I fear the additional taxation
will press heavily for the present year, and I wish it
could be avoided.” When the bold, bad Imperial Par-
Haments did not pass the Bigot Council Bills, the rate-
payers, “saved ”’ and “lost ”’ alike, have to bear the
expense, and old Mr. Edmundson, the child-like and
bland, offers them in return for their extra rates, mainly
occasioned by these unsuccessful Bills, his “ greatest sym-
pathy!”” Why should not old Mr. Edmundson and his
fellow-“ Saved,”” who run.Rathmines, be made to pay
this £10,000 out of their own pockets? They might
keep their sympathy, be it great or small, if they would
pay the piper for the tune they called.

The British Government ought to resign, for Old Mr.
Edmundson thinks that  the English Liberals made a
great mistake ’’ with regard to his Bill. Edward VII.
should call upon this Dartry Dye statesman to form a
Government without delay ! ~ Why did not Mr. Edmund-
son give a fair hearing to those who demanded a poll at
the ratopayers’ meeting held during November last?
“ T suppose,”’ said old Mr. Edmundson in, we are sure,
his most child-like and bland tone, “ I suppose there is
littlo use in reiterating that our Council is a non-political

2 :1body, composed—as anyone may ascertain who desires to

The ratepayers of Rathmines will ‘shortly have th
pleasure of paying an extra ls. 9d., or thereabouts, in
the £. Perhaps some of the ratepayers will not feel
that degree of pleasure which is fitting and proper to
the occasion, for there are ingrates even in the most re-
spectable communities. We think that the Commis-
sioners might make it even money, and pub up an extra
3d., and so make the rates 2s. higher this year, and the
proceeds of the additional 3d. should be devoted to erect-
ing a statue to old Mr. Edmundson, the child-like and
bland Chairman of the “Saved '’ Commissioners whose
paternal, and mayhap fraternal, government of Rath-
mines is now famous wherever the English language is
spoken.

The Rathmines District Council—a purely non-politi-

cal and non-sectarian body—would be a good subject for
cdmic opera ; the child-like and bland old Mr. Edmund-

heartily assure old Mr. Edmundson that he is quite cor-
rect in this—there is no¢ the slightest use in repocating
that yarn to any sonsible persons. His Council is one
of tho most, if not the most, bigoted and exclusive cor-

orations south of Portadown. The population of the
district under his Council’s jurisdiction is more than half
composed of  Idolators,” yeb by a system of two wards
and “ Tickets,”” the Council is composed of 18 “ Saved
and only three “ Idolators ’—and the three cavefully-
solected *“ Tdolators’’ are let in on the  ticket ’’ merely
to give a slight colour of liberality to the * game " that
the Rathmines Council plays. Tn the article which an-
peared in our issue of January 17th, 1903, it was shown
that this ¢ non-political and non-sectarian ’’ body had so
ordered things that out of a total of the twenty-five
botter-class jobs in the gift of the Council proper no less
than 21 were held by the “ Saved ”” in a district where
the “ Saved’’ were a numerical minority.

* So Mr. Schwartz ds going back to the place from
whence he came! It may be remembered that in our
last Christmas number we drew attontion to this gent'k’:-
man. He lived at 58 Claremont Terrace, Harold’s
Cross. Mr. Viggo Schwartz was at one timo egg and
poultry market expert to the 1.A.0.8., and from v('.ha.
house of which, according to Thom’s Directory, he was
one of the occupiers a good deal of circular touting con-
cerning Continental eggs and margarine was done.
Mr. Viggo Schwartz was subsequently cmployed by a
corporation by the name of the  Irish Fudcr;tl‘cd
Poultry Societies, Ltd.,” of which Mr. Dermot O’Brien
is president. The ‘ Irish Federated Poultry Societics,
Ltd.,” hails from No. 22 Lincolu Place, Dublin, where
the “thinkers’’ como from. As a consequence of our
revelations in our last Christmas Number tho wide-
awake directors of the Federated Poultry, ete., who,
one would think, would have known more about Mr.
Schwartz than we knew, arrived at the opinion thab it
was undesirable that the margarine and foreign egg
touting gentlemen, who were part occupiers with Mr.
Schwartz, the egg expert, of 58 Claremont Terrace,
Harold’s Cross, should ‘‘ continue to share the same
house, even as a residence, with their countryman, Mr.
Schwartz.”” The egg expert acquiesced in this opinion
to which we were told “ he will give immediate effect.’”

In our issue of December 23rd, in which we published
a letter from Mr. Dermot O’Brien, President of a
Poultry Society, we pointed out that we were in a posi-
tion to state that, as far back as the middle of the
year 1904, Viggo Schwartz contemplated taking up an
agency for a Continental firm. We received no further
communication from Mr. Dermot O’Brien, and Viggo
“kept saying nothing ’’ all the time.

Now we hear that this man is going back to
his native Denmark. What has Mr. Dermot
O’Brien, President of the Irish Federated Poultry
Societies, Ltd., 22 Lincoln Place, to say to that? We
don’t know. But we do know that the Society of which
he is president is now touting. for a testimonial for this
man! The following touting circular, headed ‘‘ Testi-
monial to Mr. V. Schwartz,” and signed “J. C.
Adams,”” whoever he is, has been sent out: —

‘“ The Directors of the Federation and the staff of the
I.A.0.8. have asked me to bring before you the fact
that Mr. Schwartz is about to leave Ireland in order
to take up an appointment in his native country (Den-
mark). It is felt that the termination of the long
period Mr. Schwartz has spent in the service of the
Irish Co-operative movement would be a very fitting
occasion for presenting him with some token of the
2oodwill and esteem in which he is held by many
friends and co-workers in this country. It is suggested
that subscriptions should not exceed the sum of one
guinea, and may be forwarded to the undersigned at
this address on or before 20th April next.”

One of the best men, by all accounts, that the
I.A.0.8. ever had in its service was Mr. P. J. Flannon,
an Irishman and, alas! an ‘‘ Idolator.”” Tle was re-
cently ““lent ”” to South Africa, and now we see that
he is to be a permanency in that place! What a com-
mentary on the hollowness, if not the virulent anti-Irish
bigotry, of these 22 Lincoln Place sort of ‘“shows’’!
Here was a man, a mere Irishman, an “‘Idolator,”
whom South Africa, which we have no reason to sup-
pose is prejudiced i favour of either Trishmen or
“Tdolators,”” grabbed up—an ITrish “Idolator’’ who
graduated in the servico of the T.A.0.8., that has some
sort of connection with “tho Department’’ to which Sir
Horace still clings. Of course, Sir Horace and the
Scotchmen would answer in reply to a query concerning
any particular importation to ‘‘the Department ’’ that
: they got the best man irrespective of nationality. Per-
- haps, Mr. P. J. Hannon, who is being grabbed up by
South Africa, is not ““ tony’’ enough for the Depart-
ment Scotch! Or, mayhap, it is not all a question of
- “tone,”’ for we understand that Mr. P. J. Hannon is
0t a Catholic of the ‘‘ tame ’” variety. Fowever, this
 a digression.

Tho Ballinasloe Branch of the Gaelic League sent an
invitation to the members of the local branch of the
Christian Young Men’s Association to take part in the
St. Patrick’s Day procession. 'l.‘he invitation' was sent
to the President of the Christian ¥oung Men, Dr.
Mills, and to onc of the Vicc-Prcsldents,_ a Mr. E.
Rothwell, who is a Clerk to the Petty Bessions. The
invitation was sent on the 14th March, and Dr. Mills
roplying stated that hc‘would not have an opportunity
of consulting his committce on the matter and so was
forced to decide for himself. No doubt it is a heavy
matter to summon a committee of the Christian Young
men of Baliinasloe! During the last regime, if word went
round that Mr. Walter Long was to arrive within three
hours, not to talk of three days, never fear the Chris-
tian Young Men, bag and baggage, committeo, Pre-
sident and all, would find a way to organise for the
occasion. In the course of Dr. Mills’ letter he wrote—
T much regret I cannot sce my way to arrange for
Representatives from the Young Men’s Christian As-
sociation to join in the procession organized by your
Branch of the Gaelic League.”” Mr. E. Rothwell, one
of the Vice-Presidents of the Christian Young Men, in
the course of his letter, wrote:—‘‘T am only one of
the Vice-Presidents of the Association and, as such, of
course, can only voice my individual opinion; and,
whilst personally second to none in my desire to honor
the memory of the Pioneer of Christianity in our
Country, and being, as I am, in full accord with the
sentiments which prompt the due observance of St.
Patrick’s Day as a public holiday, yet I do not think
it feasible that the members of the Association should
join in the Procession to which your letter refers.
From hurried enquiry which I have made I find that
most of them have made arrangements for the holiday
and Sunday following.”’

By way of comment on this letter we may reproduce
the remark made by Mr. J. J. Farrell, at a meeting of
tho Ballinasloe Gaclic League subsequent to St.
Patrick’s Day, as reported in our contemporary the
Connacht Leader:—“ We could see them standing at
the door of their Society rooms, although they had
arrangements already made for other business, accord-
ing to their Vice-President.”” Mr. Farrell also stated
that since the Branch had been formed not one Pro-
testant joined it. We do not know the local circum-
stances, but we have considerable sympathy with those
who felt that the Christian Young Men should not have
been specially invited. If no Protestant in Ballinasloe
joined the League it would point to the conclusion that
in that town, as well as generally throughout the
country, they are in the main to be. classed with
England’s Faithful Garrison, and it strikes us as lean-
ing too much toward the flabby wice of ‘‘tolerance
proving ** to oxpressly ask them. If they wanted to
join the Procession thoy were welcome. The parti-
cular Dr. Mills, who is President of the Christian Young
Men, is he of Ballinasloe who was used s a stick for
violently beating the Orange Drum seme time back.

Mr. Richard Hazleton, the member for North Gal-
way, has made his maiden speech in the British Parlia-
ment. Our readers who have read the many articles
which he contributed to the LEeAaDER will not be sur-
prised to learn that he acquitted himself well.  The
following from the Parliamentary notes in the York-
shire Post (a Unionist paper, we believe,) will be read

with interest: —

“The scconder of the motion proved himself an interesting young man,
and an_undoubted acquisition to the House. Mr. Hazclton fought Mr
Walter Long in Dublin, and for his prowess on’ that occasion ‘was re.
warded with the first Nationalist seat that fell vacant in the distressful
country. To-diy be was put forward by his Olympian master, Mr. John
Redmond, to siow his mettle, and he roso to his opportunity with a ¢ ol
ness a grace, and a Parlinmentary aptitude that are rare in theso duys
on the Irish benohes. Members, Unionist as well as Radical, even came in
to staud at the Bar of the House to listen to him. Lord Rathmore (beiter
known ns Mr. David Plunkett in the old days, when he was no mean orator
himself) leaned over the front of the Peers’ Gllery and followed the specch
with cager appreciation. Mr. Hazelfon, when Fe can do o well in his
smooth-faced youth, ought to make his mark at Westminster. The Wons:

We are not surprised that Mr. Hazleton made such
a good impression, for the contributors to the Luapur
are usually of one quality—the best. We hope that
Mr. Hazleton will keep a sharp look on the Bills of the
Great Northern Bigot Line, and we are sure that if
Brother Goulding should want the British Parliament
to oblige his Railway in any matter that the member
for North Galway will display commendable activity.

In an English paper called the Bolton News, of
March 22nd, the captions ‘ Roman Catholicism in Tre-
land. Work of the Irish Church Mission ’’ suggest
entertaining matter to the Trish *“ Idolatrous’’ reader.
The Rev. Mr. Herseo presided over the annual meeting
of the ““ Bolton Auxiliary of the Trish Church Missions
to Roman Catholics.”” Amongst thoso present was a
Mr. B. ITare, from Limerick, whoover he is. _The Rov.
Phillip Johnson harangued the meeting. In such a
harangue the word ‘‘Devolution’’ and the phrase
« Protestant minority,”” of course, occur. Tlope f(,J,l' us
poor benighted, obscurant, ignorant, ‘‘ Tdolators’’ of
Treland springs eternal in the ‘‘saved ’’ breasts. Rev.
Mr. Johnson said: —‘‘On the other hand it was thought
by some that as the Roman clorgy began to wield
greater powers in Treland that a number would become

_aggressive and domineering, and that their spirit would

alionato the people, and there were not wanting signs
that this was coming to pass in parts.”’

Mr. B. Hare, from Limerick, then entertained this
meeting at ““saved ’’ Bolton. Mr. Hare, speaking of
the difficulties of the work in the open-air in Ireland,
said his body had often been tossed up and down in an
Ivish fair like a shubtlecock.  There is, our obscurant
readers will observe, no lack of verisimilitude about that
yarn! Mr. Hare, of Limerick, whoever he is, called
upon the people of Bolton to “ Awake! the enemy is in
the camp.”” As we will presently see it is to be hoped
that they will soon awake—to their own state! Of
course, he had much to say about the Souper dispensary
carried on by the man Long in Limerick. Said Mr.
IHare—*‘ There were many conversions through the
work in the dispensary, and the Church of Rome was
being undermined in Limerick. The people of Ireland
were all right, but the teaching was all wrong; purga-
tory being the fire which made the pot of the priests
boil.””  We are told after all this that the meceting
closed with the Benediction !

In his opening remarks the chairman stated that—
‘“The present time was one of peculiar anxicty as to
the future of Ireland, and he wanted to urge upon the
hearts of English people the supreme importance of
remembering Ireland increasingly in prayer.” Might
we urge upon the heart of the Bolton people, where this
meeting was held, the supreme importance of Bolton
examining its own conscience. For in the very same
issue of the Bolton News, which contains a report of
Mr. Hare’s shuttlecock tale, we find some news under
the heading ‘“ Bolton’s Criminal Returns.”” The re-
port, we learn, shows ‘ that during 1905, 689 indictable
crimes have been reported to the police, an increase of
49 on the previous year.  Proceedings were taken
against 395 persons (313 males and 82 females, 349
being apprehended without warrant, 29 on warrant,
and 17 summoned. There was an increase of 32 persons
proceeded against for indictable offences compared with
1904. TFor non-indictable offences, 2,592 persons (2,028
males and 564 females) were dealt with, an increase
of 79, drunkenness being the offence in which the
greatest increase was shown. There were 917 applica-
tions, followed by 756 orders under the Married
Women’s Act, and affiliation and breaches of the peace.
On information received from distant police authorities,
56 persons were apprehended and duly handed over.””
We further learn that 72 juveniles (65 males and 7
females) were dealt with for felony, and 25 (13 males
and 12 females) for other offences. The police force
numbered only 177. Might we suggest that the Bolton
people, beside examining their own consciences, should
increase the number of their police.  Mr. Hare, of
Limerick, as well as the man Long, might transfer
their labowrs from the Shannon city to Bolton of the
seventy-two juvenile felons. Tustead of spending time
and monoy In this country in the endeavour to make
Soupers and Jumpers, could they not find plenty of
employment in Bolton, not to talk of the Potteries and
other parts of “saved” England? But work on the
“ saved " might not fetch tho money out of the pockets
of the credulous old women.

“What are Bthics?’ one might picture a  Trinity !
bigot asking as he turned on his heel, not waiting for
an answer. Dynamics are, however, beginning to
frighten some of them. The decline of the roll of
students at the Souper University of the mud-throwing
cads, the advent of & “Liberal Government.” in Eng-
land, and tho various effective assaults, including that

which his Grace of Dublin i we write, making upon
Bngland’s Faithful Fortress in College Green, are begin-
" ning to tell. The Fortress could chuckle at mere logic;

but dynamic_forces are things the squeezes of which

even' Anthony Bigot, the Provost, must wince under,
We see that one of the Professors of the mud-throwers
University, Mr. E. P. Culverwell, has addressed a lettor
to the papers. In the course of it he gives the following
nasty rap to the old men who draw the enormous amount
of money that has excited even the cupidity of the philo-
sophic Mr. Puck:— As to the system of government
by the Provost and the seven Senior Fellows, it stands
condemned already. To that anachronism it is due that
the irregularitios’ of which Archbishop Walsh is now
making so much were porsisted in, after the repeated
warnings of the General Medical Council, and there aro
other ways in which wo still suffer from the supineness
of ‘our aged rulers.’”  “ Trinity ’ must be in a bit of &
fright when its Mr. Culverwell writes publicly of “the
supineness of our aged rulers.”

‘The heroic “ Hands off Trinity ’’ war whoop has its
drawbacks, as Mr. Culverwell clearly sees. “No
doubt,” he says, ““ there is an alternative policy which
Trinity College and its friends might adopt—‘ Hands off
Trinity.” But whither will it lead? With the sale of
landed estates the classes which have hitherto fed the
University must continue their steady decline, etc.”
Evidently the time is coming when some of the gallant
yeomen of the mud-throwers’ University will take kindly
$o a “Hands up ”’ policy.

After all, Trinity College is a sort of nightmare to this
unfortunate country. Why not wipe it out altogether,
and commence on the new! Why not utilise part of the
buildings for carrying on the business of the Bank of
Ireland, the present premises of which may be wanted
at “no far distant date.”” The remaining floor space of
the College could possibly be turned to account for the

. purposes of a picture gallery and a museum. What Mr.
Culverwell calls ““ the supineness of our aged '’ Provost
and Senior Fellows might be transferred for a few years
to the governing body of the museum. No persons
bother much about museums, and so the Provost and
the seven Senior Fellows could be supine to their hearts’
content in their work of pinning beetles and arranging
mummies without drawing upon them the attention of a
busy public.  Jobs could probably be found for the
Junior Fellows as bank clerks, and the Nutting scholars
could be emigrated to Canada or some other British
eclony.

There is an eating corporation by the name of tho
Corinthian Club (or Grub)—why Corinthian we do not
know—in Mudlin by the Liffey shore. Sir Charles
Camoron—an imported Scotchman we understand— is
president of it, and one W. A. Craig, J.P., a composer

. of doggerel, is Hon. Treasurer. The musical director is
Mr. T. R. G. Jozé, who is also “ grand organist ’ of the

Castlecaldwell is a village on the shore of Lough Erne,
consisting of a Police Barrack and a Railway Station.
In the immediate neighbourhood reside a number of
Tdolators who are half a century in advance of resenting
injuries by passing resolutions. Petbigo is their market,
town, and hither they were wont to travel by the Great
Northern Bigot Line, until an official of that line thought
well to wipe his boots in their political sent‘ments. They
sought redress, but in vain. Only a few wecks have
passed since then, but within those few weeks a number
of well-appointed two-horse brakes have been put on the
road between Castlecaldwell and Pettigo, and carry the
farmers and their luggage to and from the markst at a
cheaper rate than the G. N. Bigot Railway carried them
heretofore. Arrangements (alike satisfactory to farmers
and brake-owners) have, we understand, been made te
continue this practical little lesson to the G. N. R.

GREAT NORTHERN RAILWAY.

~ HE Leaper has shown again and again that the
Great Northern Railway is one of those public
Corporations which exclude Catholics, with very few ex-
ceptions, from its service, and especially from its better-
paid positions. At the half-yearly meeting held in
Amiens Street on the 14th February, this matter was
brought prominently forward. —There was a very large
attendaunce ; the advocates of equality for all creeds and
classes had all the arguments on their side; and when
the voting was taken it seemed to us that they had also
the majority of votes. The Chairman ruled, however,
that  the noes had it.”’

The matter has now been transferred to the House
of Commons. The Great Northern wishes to have three
Bills passed. One of these would transfer the Donegal
Railways to the Great Northern (Ireland) and the Great
Midland (England). As far as we know clerkships on
the Donegal railways are at present open to persons of
all denominations, but if these railways be transferred
to the Great Northern, then practically “ Na Catholic
need apply.”” ~ We are therefore much pleased to see
that Mr. MacVeigh, Mr. P. O’Brien, Mr. Swift Mac-
Neill and Mr. M‘Kean have blocked the three Bills of
the Great Northern, “ pending some satisfactory under-
taking being given by the Great Northern Company as
to the exclusion of Catholics from clerkships in its
service.”’

Mr. MacVeigh, besides blocking these Bills, intends
to still further ventilate this important matter by asking
the President of the Board of Trade “whether his atten-
tion has been called to the political and religious intoler-
ance shown by Irish railway companies in their appoint-
ments, and whether, seeing that these companies derive
their monopoly from Parliament, he will take steps to
ensuro that they will exercise their patronage im-
partially.”’

Railway companies are by no means the only offenders
in this respect. ~Many of the banks are just as bad,
perhaps worso. Like railway companies banking com-
panies receivo special privileges. These privileges are
given by Parliament—which represonts all sections of
the community—for the general good; but many banks
and many railways carefully exclude Catholics from a
due share of their patronage.

Catholics only ask for equality of treatment. They
ask for no favours; but thoy are awakening to the fach
that hitherto they have got nothing like fair play, and
that as a consequenco they have to leave this country for
want of employment. We are much pleased at the par-
liamentary action taken by Messrs. MacVeigh, O’Brien,
MacNeill and M‘Kean, and we aro confident that it will
be cnergetically supported by all who wish to seo fair
play dealt out to all creeds and classes. In Ireland the

vast power which Parliament has conferred on railway

A

corporations generally falls into the hands of a small !

clique of directors, and a cardinal principle with these
seems to be to exclude Catholics from all the well-pai

Marcn 31, 1906. "

appointments.  In the Great Northern the fact that
Catholics form a large portion of the sharcholders, and
that Catholic customers contribute fully half the income
of the line, is forgotten or contemptuously ignored by
the directors; and i‘t now rests with the Irish Parlia-
mentary Tepresentatives to insist on the insertion in the
company’s Bills of a clause which will secure equality of
treatment for all. If the bigotry and intolerance of the
directors will not permit them willingly to agreo to this,
the sad fate of the Rathmines Bill, which was promoted
by_a. Corporation quite as exclusive and as bigoted as any
l‘aul.\va.y Board, may convince them that it will be in
their shareholders’ interest to act fairly towards all
denominations. S,

THE CASE AGAINST TRINITY COLLEGE.

i CANNOT say that I have had any enthusiasm
| stirred in me by the Trinity College debate and its
| result—the appointment.of a Commission.  Whether
| through want of time or other cause, the statement of
our case by the Irish members was not particularly effec-
tive, and I doubt whether the Commission will be very
offective either. It is, no doubt, something gained that
Trinity College should no longer be officially treated as
an impregnable stronghold of educational perfection,
but it is scarce likely to succumb to a reconnaissance of
the type suggested.  That institution has, on various
occasions, been subjected to exaggerated praise, and on
others to exaggerated and insincere censure of the “sour-
grapes *’ variety, but the real facts about it are by no
means mysterious. It provides an excellent education
of a somewhat antiquated kind for middle-class Episco-
palian Protestants who care to work, and a reputable
degree on easy terms for those who prefer to idle. Its
large endowments enable it to do the former; its his-
{ toric associations the latter.

Without any Commission at all its good and bad
points are fairly well known, and may be summed up
| in a few simple propositions:—

(@) Its Honour candidates reach a really high standard

i in Classics (of a somewhat unliterary type) and Mathe-

| matics. This results from its Fellowship examination
being practically confined to those two subjects.

(b) Dowden and one or two others maintain a good

standard among its students in English literature.

| (¢) It has a successful Engincering School.

TIts studies in Science—whether pure or applied—
and in Philosophy are beneath contempt.

i - (e) It affords no serious encouragement to the study

i of History, Political Economy, Irish, or Modern Lan-

" guages. The results are correspondingly poor.

| (f) Its Medical Degrees are, especially as compared

with those of the R.U.I., of very low standard.

{ : (9) Its Law School and Law Examinations are a farce.

(L) The standard exacted for its Pass degrees in Arts,
especially as compared with that of the R.U.T., is very
low.

(s) Its elder Fellows, such as Tyrrell and Mahaffy,
have, in many cases, done valuable original work. The
younger ones compete in barrenness with those of the
R.U.I

(7) It resembles Oxford and Cambridge, and differs
from most of the other Universitics of Turope in being
a residential university—a “ cloister,”” as the Germans
would style it.

. (k) It is much inferior to Oxford and Cambridge in
the antelleciual intercourse of its students, but has a good
deal of University life of an athletic character.

(?) Like other University institutions in Ireland, it
40t affords no encouragement, for original rescarch.

e () Its fees are ’11i§h , and consequently it is frequented

by a somewhat wealthier type of bourgeois than the

R.U.I. Protestants of the upper classes, Catholics and

Prosbyterians alike boycott it. Of these classes of stu-

_ donts less than one in ten frequent ib.

(n) The lotus-eating eoxistence of ils seven senile
Scnior Fellows, who draw an immense salary for the easy

“task of mismanaging it, makes its Fellowship—which

glves a distant right of succession to these posts—a

ighly respectablo pogition much sought after,

'THE LEADER.

(0) It is hopelessly anti-national. Bl

(p) It is, in practice, entirely confined to the Church
of Ireland. i e

() In addition to being a teaching body, it also fulfils
tho function of a mere examining Board, and gives a cer<
tain proportion (11'6 per cent. ab the lowest estimate) of
its Arts degrees by oxamination only, like the Royal
University.

This, I believe—apart from the Divinity School—is
almost all that is to be said or to be found out aboub
Trinity College.

Now, that being so, what are we to think about the
Commission?  In one way, of course, it is good. It
will frighten Trinity College a little, and perhaps make
it more amenable. But it will do very little more. For it
is likely to be prejudiced in favour of Trinity College,
especially if it contains any considerable element of Ox-
ford or Cambridge graduates. A continental professor
would give you. a fairer verdict (and I think the Irish
Party ought to press for the inclusion of continental
members), but the mere sight of the cricket-crease will
be enough to win the approval of your Oxford man. The
Commission will point out a few of the weak points I
havo referred to. It will give exaggerated praise to the
College’s Classical and Mathematical achievements,
though it will probably recommend the adoption
of the Oxford tutorial system of instruction. It
will suggest further endowment for scientific study.
It will propose that the governing body be
chosen by election instead of seniority. (Every-
body in Trinity wants this). It may widen the Fellow-
ship course, and make some other reforms in detail,
which will strengthen and improve Trinity College.. Et
done bon jour! Where do the 920 out of 1,000 existing
Catholic students who do not enter Trinity College come
in? Where does the University education of the Irish
people come? Even if the Divinity School be removed
into Nassau St. or Westland Row, will that alter
matters?

The truth is, that a Commission can only suggest that
the Trinity monopoly be mended, while Ireland needs
that it be ended. If, of course, the governing body of
Trinity were to be made to be elected—say, by the
County Councils—it could be made suitable to Catholics
quick enough. Or if a number of Catholic pro-
fessors equal to the existing number of Protes-
tants were to be appointed on the staff, and
made electors of the governing body, it would,
if mnot mado suitable to Catholics, at least be
brought into harmony with the principles of the Liberal
Government. But that either of these courses should
be cither adopted or recommended seems excessively un-

They finally decided to give them a fair trial. But very
soon a persistent and insidious attempt was made to turn
these also into seminaries of souperism. Cardinal Cullen
fought that from 1850 till the day he died; and if he
had not left them quite satisfactory from a Catholic
point of view, he had, at least, killed their power for

gious element in those schools, because the Castle autho-
rities tried every dodge to poison the faith of the people
through them, and the Bishops had to conct_mbmte their
defonco on the point attacked. According to Mr.
Davitt’s principles they should have remained dumb
watch-dogs whilst the Castle authorities were using those

few. Mr. Davitt is not satisfied with the secular educa-

By the Mr. Davitts of the last fifty years? No; the

first improvement was forced on the “ National Board *’
by Cardinal Cullen, although as to being a member of

read that part of his evidence before the Powis Com-
mission bearing on the un-Irish character of those lesson-
books, and he will learn a lesson in Irish history which,
I believe, he badly needs.

Mr. Davitt is dissatisfied with these books, and with
the secular education provided by the “ National schools’”
generally.' Very well, then. What has he ever done to
improve either the one or the other? Can anyone who
reads this call to mind any one act or word ho has done
or spoken to improve either? I don’t know of one. All
I know of his action in tho matter is, that he has mado
_buudles of speeches and written acres of letters denounc-
ing t,hosq who have at least tried to do their best. Had
he, for instance, stayed in Parliament like other Irish
members who are quito as patriotic as ho is and a good
dzal more practical, he might have helped towards some
improvement in those matters. He might have helped
on Home Rule, or at least somo partial romedial mea-
sures for the industrial benefit of the country. No; his
humanitarian conscience would not let him stay in Par-
liament, and ho instead went off to South Africa and
wrote a book about the Boers, whose very conduct to-
wards their country should have been an object-lesson to
him in his duty towards his own. It is much easier to
sit on a ditch abusing Irish bishops and laying down the
law for Irish Parliamentarians than to take a man’s
share in the educational and national work which they
are, each in his own sphere, doing their best to carry out.
It would seem as though Mr. Davitt’s heart was bleeding
over the industrial and educational condition of Ireland.
Now, I have never heard that he has started, or taken
any part in starting, for instance, a Young Men’s Club,
a Technical School, a local industry of any kind. I
have not heard that he has ever started a branch of the
Gaelic League in Dalkey; that he ever became a member
of the Gaelic League; that he has ever spoken or written
in support of it; that he has in any way identified him-
self with the Gaelic movement. He despises the Catho-
lic Truth Society and deplores the literary garbage im-
ported into Ireland. Very well; has he offered any im-
provement or substitute for the former, or has he sug-
gested any practical plan to prevent the latber? T shall
be glad to be corrected, if I am mistaken. By the way,
during the last twenty-five years has he ever taken an
active part in the work of a Land League or National
League 7n the locality where he lived? I do not in the
least doubt his desire for his country’s welfare; but his
work in that direction has largely consisted in finding
fault with the work of others who at least do their best.
Tt gives me no pleasure to write thus of a man whom in
many rzspaets I greatly admire; but the times are
changed, and the time has come for talking straight to
everybody, social philosophers not excepted.

Let me now write this as plainly as I can—and Mr.
Davitt knows it as well as I do—7"le Irish Bislops are
no more responsiile for the stale of seculur education
provided by the “ National”’ school system than he .
They had no voice in the framing of it, and they arc
powerless to change it. Mr. Davitt also sneers at the
Catholic colleges connected with the Intermediate sys-
“tem, and implicitly inculpates the ecclesiastical organisa-
tion in the country. Neither the  National,”” nor the
Intermediate system was framed by Irish Catholic occle-
giastics ; nor have they any power to alter those systems.
They have had only to take them as they came, and work
under them till better times. Yet here arc a few facts:
—The National schools under nuns and Christian Bro-
thers, although attended by the poorest of the poor, have
beaten the Model schools in which Mr. Davitt's ideal of
secular education is complete, and in which the well-to-
do are taught. The Catholic colleges have beaten the
non-Catholic colleges, and even the Queen’s Colleges in
which Mr. Davitt’s ideal of secular education is complete.

THE LEADER.

Marca 31, 1906.

Now, what about ecclesiastical disregard for secular edu-
cation ?

Having placed these remarks before the public, each
can, for himself, st his own value on the following ele-
gant extract which I take from one of Mr. Davitt's let-
tems:—

“ Wake up, my Lord Bishop of Limerick, and look
around you in your own country. Let Irishmen in Eng-
land mind their own concerns, which they are well qua-
lified to do. [Yet Mr. Davitt went over and inter-
fered!] What about our ‘National’ schools and the
bost and highest teaching elements in the pedagogue
world, if rightly paid and fairly trained and justly
treated, as they are not under a system which you up-
hold ; a system which is enclosed inside a kind of deno-
minational compound and fed upon the mental pabulum
of the ‘ National ’ Board and Catholic Truth Society.
Aro thero no changes needed here? Is it * a betrayal of
religion ’ to suggest that a system of popular education
under which Ireland is flooded with the lowest and most
demoralising kind of English literature, containing the
seeds of the worst kind of materialistic unbelief ; a sys-
tem saturated with the dencminational spirit ; has some
indircet bearing upon this unwelcome tide of moral rub-
bish now sweeping in upon our shores?”’

Now, twenty years ago that would be popularly called
“fine writing.”” But it will be a shock to many that it
was necessary for Mr. Davitt to wake up his lazy Lord-
ship of Limerick to fight the “ National *’ Board and sys-
tem; I thought he hardly ever left the “ ring,”’ against
that Board and system. Mr. Davitt’s heart bleeds for
the training of teachers; his lazy Lordship of Limerick
has built a magnificent training college, which within
the few years of its existence has won prestige. When
his lazy Lordship was only a curate he took a leading
part in building splendid schools for a neglected guarter
of Limerick; yes, Mr. Davitt, and artisans’ dwellings;
and worked a Temperance Hall for working men, and a
Literary Institute for the young men of Limerick. Let
me ask again what has Mr. Davitt ever done, or tried to
do, for any of those classes, in any of those spheres—He
has made speeches! Written letters by the acre! Has
triumphantly proved that what others have done could
be done much better!! Mis lazy Lordship about ten
years ago fought for and secured Leamy’s school for the
poor of Limerick for whose use it was left, but which
the Protestants had misappropriated for the benefit of
their own well-to-do children, for about seventy years—
But Mr. Davitt would condemn him there of over active
sectarianism! Of course, sectarianism for non-conform-
ists or Secularists is justice; justice for Catholics is sec-
tarianism! God bless us all. %

THE R.H.A. EXHIBITION.

HAVE scen better shows than this year’s Exhibition
at the Royal Hibernian Academy. I shall not
now discuss the question whether the Academy is going
down, but I feel sccure in saying that it does not seem
to be going up. Thero is plenty of carcful, artistic work
to be seen on its walls, but not much that bespeaks the
higher moods and greater moments of the artist’s life.
As usual, landscape and portraiture predominate, and
in these lines there are not many changes remaining to
be rung, or if there b2, they are not rung here. Taken
all round, the show, though decent, is not precisely
stimulating. None of the big wigs of Burlington Touse
show anything this year, except the inevitable
M‘Whirter, who has only one piece, and that not a case
of unum sed leonem cither—for it is a donkey. * Indeed,
the absence of anything striking and notable from the
Academy walls this year is all the more depressing when
one thinks of the recent Watts Exhibition in these very
rooms. Not only is there no important work shown by
any R.A., but there is none by any other outsider
either, nor any by the R.H.A.’s themselves. Still the
show will repay a visit to Abbey St., and if there is
nothing wonderfully novel or important, there is at least
meritorious work, and a grateful absence of rubbish and
impossible stuff.

Mr. J. B. Yeats shows several portraits, some of them
marked by his characteristic merits, but all of them
streaked with his usual defects. Sir Joshua Reynolds
used to get his pupils or assistants to do the draperi
and details of costume. Mr. Yeats should do something
like that, too. He either can not, or positively w2l not,
paint the commonest details of costume decently and
rightly. Look at his portrait of Mr. Standish O’Grady.
As usual he gets the expression of the face, and particu-

larly of the eyes, right; no one needs to be told that it -

is Standish O’Grady. But look at the linen collar and
the cuffs! If any man turned up at a garden party or
a five o’clock tea with that collar and those cuffs he would
surely be asked no more. I know it is hard to manage
these things in a picture; the hardness of outline, and
the white glare of the linen, have an inartistic, or rather
an unazsthetic, effect. Still, somehow, other artists
manage the matter fairly enough; Mr. Yeats always
makes a gentleman’s linen look as if the rats had been
gnawing the edges of it, and as if it had last been laun-
dered with a soap of the ““ won’t wash clothes”’ variety.
Again, look at his portrait of Mr. Harrington, M.P., in
his robes as Lord Mayor of Dublin. See how the fur of
the robes is handled, and look at that round, yellow
thing hanging out of the chain of office! What is it?
Verily if Northcote had done his work after this fashion,
Sir Joshua would very soon have sent him to the right-
about. There is a Reynolds in the Mansion House,
Dublin—a portrait of a Lord Lieutenant of Treland,
hanging, I think, in the drawing-room. Let Mr. Yeats
step in and study that picture, and then cross over to
Abbey St., and look at his own! Certainly, I hope that
that thing he intends for a portrait of Mr. T. Harring-
ton will never be hung in the Mansion House, or in the
City Ifall; if it De, it will not do credit to its company,
or to Mr. Yeats’s undoubted abilities. “I once did
hold it baseness to write fair,”’ says Hamlet, and Mr.
Yeats quite evidently “holds it baseness’’ to give his
pictures anything like artistic finish of detail. ~ More
is the pity, seeing how much ability the artist certainly
has. Miss Mary Swanzy’s portrait of “ H. R. Swanzy,
Esq.,” is a good piece of work, and looks a convincing
portrait.  Another woman artist, Miss Purser, has
hardly done so well by Signor Esposito (No. 68). It
